title: buildah mount ctr in rootless mode should work.
body: We should enter the usernamespace before mounting and it would work fine.  If we do similar with other commands we should be able to get most of buildah commands to work.

comments.body:
```
$ buildah mount alpine-working-container
cannot mount using driver overlay in rootless mode
ERRO[0000] exit status 1                            
```
```
$ buildah unshare
# buildah mount alpine-working-container
/home/dwalsh/.local/share/containers/storage/overlay/bcef5f32e15f3dc982bd1239c03030a23a29fbb941a86ba211252acc8a660d92/merged
exit
```
```
$ touch /home/dwalsh/.local/share/containers/storage/overlay/bcef5f32e15f3dc982bd1239c03030a23a29fbb941a86ba211252acc8a660d92/merged/dan
```
```
 buildah umount alpine-working-container
472be9b00a55524077005980063b8dcd786494f5a0fab84ceefeb9a76fb2b675
```

the issue is that the same user/mount namespace must be maintained through all the session, not only during `buildah mount`, so doing the `buildah unshare` before is necessary for properly setting it up.

Differently than Podman where we manage to join the namespace from podman itself, it won't work with Buildah as we allow external tools to access and manage the storage, so I don't see any other way than wrapping the entire session with `buildah unshare` and work from there.

Differently `build-using-dockerfile` is fine as it wraps internally the rootless session.

So can we either change buildah mount to reexec itself as
```
buildah mount fedora-working-container
buildah unshare buildah mount fedora-working-container
```
that will break the non interactive use case: `ctr=$(buildah mount fedora-working-container)` as buildah mount will re-exec and create the `unshare` session

Hello!

+1 for invoking unshare silently in mount in rootless mode, because non-interactive case already broken:
    
    id=$(buildah from centos)
    mount=$(buildah unshare buildah mount ${id})

returns:

    Main config did not have a override_install_langs attr. before setopt
    Main config did not have a override_install_langs attr. before setopt
    Error: This command has to be run under the root user.

Fedora 29 and buildah-1.6-2.git5f95bd9.fc29.x86_64

@giuseppe oh, I see, like that it worked:

    buildah unshare ./build.sh

Thant you!

**P.S.** But imho, it would be nice not to bother about different tricks and rootless mode worked out of the box.
@giuseppe please, don't get me wrong, I'm just saying from perspective of user which I'm now :-)

I think we need to fix this via documentation.  In rootless mode buildah mount should throw and error explainign how you need to do this in rootless mode.
```
$ buildah mount $ctr
Error: Buildah mount in rootless mode needs to be run within a buildah unshare
session.

First execute
# buildah unshare
# buildah mount $ctr
...
# buildah commit $ctr image
# exit

Alternatively you can create a bash script and execute it from `buildah unshare ./buildscript.sh`
```
@SergeyBear I agree with you people are stumbling over this, and we at least need to make it clear what is going on.

we already have an error when trying to mount overlay directly without an `unshare` environment, and we have also the documentation for it: https://github.com/containers/buildah/commit/b4f712fa756aeb10169d4b43eee83edc894d1604

I've opened another PR to improve the error message: https://github.com/containers/buildah/pull/1339

So is it possible, rootlessly, to use mount in non-interactive scripts (in arbitrary languages), somehow saving the environment created by `unshare`, so that it can explicitly be provided to other (potentially non-buildah) operations? It seems the `env` vars are not sufficient, so what else can be "exported" of the created environment so that a separate "session" may also access the mounted filesystem?

You can just mount the same mount point in a different session
buildah unshare
buildah mount ctr
Go to town.

@rhatdan Thanks, but I'm thinking of a case that doesn't totally support sessions in the sense of multiple commands directly affecting each other's "environments". What defines the environment (or is that the wrong question)?
I think you really need to describe what you want to do.
For example, certain cases of using a configuration management tool like Ansible making multiple SSH connections to run various steps, some of which need access to the mounted container filesystem.

Access will be controlled via standard Linux protections. The mount point is defined within the tool, you can not mount to arbitrary locations, although you could bind mount the mount point to other locations.  once the image is mounted any process on the system can use it as long as it is in the same mount namespace and can see the mountpoint,  governed by DAC/User permissions.

Thank you for the quick explanation!

The mount location as-is is no problem. Somehow I missed the ways unshare can be used to run any arbitrary process. Is there a good and non-evil way to pack the unshare command into the shebang? Something like

```bash
#!/bin/env/buildah unshare -- /bin/env python3
```

Why not create a command called bunshare

```
#!/bin/sh
exec $@
```
bunshare /bin/env python3

Well I'm looking to make scripts directly executable, and also able to mount containers. I'd rather not add extra files or path manipulations or special instructions on executing if not strictly necessary. 

And either I'm really misunderstanding the suggestion, or you left out `buildah unshare` altogether.

Yes I guess my example should have been
exec buildah unshare -- $@


Upstream buildah unshare now has support for --mount CTRID.

FWIW, this works with `env` from modern GNU coreutils:

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env -S buildah unshare -- python3
```
